title: Distributed Systems
description: Examines the principles and design of distributed computing systems including distributed algorithms, consensus protocols, fault tolerance, replication, distributed file systems, and peer-to-peer architectures.

title: Statistical Methods in Artificial Intelligence
description: Covers statistical foundations of AI and machine learning including probability theory, Bayesian inference, regression, classification, dimensionality reduction, and probabilistic graphical models.

title: Design Thinking 101 – Research to Define
description: Outcomes (COs) The overall goal of design thinking course is to help design better solutions, products, services, systems, processes, strategies, and experiences. This course is aimed at guiding through the Design Thinking Process and will help developing a solid understanding of the overall process, phases and methods in design thinking. Introduce the concept of Human-centred approach, empathy, collaboration, co-creation and product-user & product-market fit. It will provide the theory and oper

title: Design Thinking – Idea to Evaluate/Implement
description: Outcomes (COs) This course is the extension of the earlier course “Design Thinking101-Research to Define” and will introduce the knowledge and skills required for the second diamond of the overall design thinking process. This course is aimed at guiding the students to work through the Ideation & Prototyping (Diversion) and Test/Evaluate (Convergence) phases of the second diamond of the overall Design Thinking Process. This course will help the student appreciating the criticality and value of g

title: Human-Computer Interaction
description: UNIT1.Introduction to Human-Computer Interaction (Week 1- Lecture1) • Introduction to HCI? • History of HCI • How Human interact with outside world? • Human Conceptual/Mental models • Conﬂict between Mental Models and Design Models. UNIT2.UNDERSTANDINGHuman-Machine System (Week1-Lecture 2) • Understanding Human-Machine System • Human-Action Cycle (HAC) • 7stagesofHuman-action cycle. • Classroom exercise on HAC • User Experience UNIT3.Art and Science of User Experience and UI Design (Week2 -Lectu

title: Product Management 101
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Early-Stage Funding for a Startup
description: This is an introductory course for understanding popular funding options available for an early- stage startup, steps required to make the startup fundable, typical expectations of startup investors and an approach to valuation for such funding. Course Outcomes :

title: Business Fundamentals
description: The course introduces business fundamentals, processes and terminology to non-business students. An understanding of the legal structures (incorporation models), business models, marketing methods and practices, principles of accounting and finance. The students are introduced to the process of business model development through case studies. The methods and approaches offered in this Business Fundamentals course are aimed at improving the capacity of cross-functional teams to collaborate and cr

title: Product Marketing
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Modern Complexity Theory
description: Covers advanced topics in computational complexity including complexity classes, reductions, circuit complexity, randomised computation, interactive proofs, and the P vs NP problem landscape.

title: Entropy and Information
description: Introduces information theory covering entropy, mutual information, channel capacity, source and channel coding theorems, data compression, and applications in cryptography and machine learning.

title: Quantum Information Theory
description: Explores the intersection of quantum mechanics and information theory, covering qubits, quantum gates, entanglement, quantum algorithms (Grover's, Shor's), quantum error correction, and quantum key distribution protocols.

title: Hardware for AI
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Advanced Computer Networks
description: Explores advanced networking concepts including network protocols, routing algorithms, software-defined networking, network security, and performance analysis of modern communication systems.

title: Blockchain and Web3 Development
description: Introduces blockchain fundamentals, consensus mechanisms, smart contract development using Solidity, decentralised applications (DApps), and emerging Web3 technologies including DeFi and NFTs.

title: Data Analytics I
description: Covers data mining and analytics techniques including data preprocessing, classification, clustering, association rule mining, and regression, with hands-on lab projects using Python-based analytics toolkits.

title: Information Retrieval and Extraction
description: Covers techniques for retrieving and extracting information from large text corpora including indexing, ranking models, evaluation metrics, named entity recognition, relation extraction, and neural IR methods.

title: Topics in Software Engineering
description: The course will cover the following topics:  Art of writing research papers,  Software Aging  Introduction to software architecture  Software architectural framework and patterns  Applying AI for software engineering practices,  Introduction to self-adaptive systems  Applying ML for self-adaptation, microservices and serverless architectures,  Software product lines  Software reengineering  Software evolution

title: Digital Image Processing
description: Covers fundamentals and advanced topics in digital image processing including spatial and frequency domain filtering, image segmentation, morphological operations, feature extraction, and deep learning-based vision.

title: Advanced Natural Language Processing
description: Covers advanced NLP techniques including language models, sequence-to-sequence models, attention mechanisms, transformers, and applications in machine translation, summarization, and question answering.

title: Mobile Robotics
description: Covers autonomous mobile robot systems including kinematics, sensor fusion, SLAM, motion planning, obstacle avoidance, and deep learning-based perception for real-world robotic applications.

title: Causal Inference
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Research in Information Security
description: Focuses on research methodologies in information security covering cryptographic protocols, network and system security, formal verification of security protocols, and critical analysis of contemporary security research literature.

title: Social Science Perspective on Human-Computer Interaction
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Environmental Science and Technology
description: Explores environmental issues, ecosystem science, and IT-enabled solutions for sustainability, covering pollution, climate change, biodiversity, green computing, and environmental modelling.

title: Design for Social Innovation
description: Explores human-centered design methodologies applied to socially impactful technology solutions, integrating design thinking, stakeholder analysis, prototyping, and community engagement for innovation.

title: User Research Methods
description: Introduces qualitative and quantitative user research methods for UX design, covering interviews, surveys, usability testing, field studies, and data synthesis to inform human-centered design decisions.

title: Digital VLSI Design
description: Introduction to digital design, Digital design metrics (Performance, Power, Functionality, Robustness, etc.) and their discussion in general, why low power, why high performance, Power-delay optimal designs, why technology scaling, issues in state-of-the-art digital designs i.e., making modern digital circuits, corner-based nanoscale design, statistical circuit design. Combinational IC design, Sequential IC design, Role of CAD tools, RTL design, Logic Synthesis, Logic Simulations, Static Timing

title: CMOS References and Regulators
description: Introduction to Internet-of-Things, Various analog systems in IoT, review of important concepts like op- amp design. PVT variations, resistor and BJT based PTAT and CTAT generating elements, temperature compensation, defining specifications of voltage reference line regulation, PSRR, accuracy, process-spread, etc., Brokaw bandgap reference, variations of Brokaw bandgap reference, op-amp based bandgap references, op-amp less bandgap references, design procedure for bandgap references, sub-bandgap

title: Robotics: Dynamics and Control
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Signal Detection and Estimation Theory
description: Estimation Theory - Minimum Variance Unbiased (MVU) Estimation, Cramer Rao lower bound, Fisher Information, Neyman-Fisher factorization, Sufficient and complete test statistics, Rao- Blackwell-Lehmann-Scheffe theorem. Estimation methods - Best linear unbiased estimation (BLUE), Least square estimation (LSE), Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), Bayesian Approach, Numerical methods - Newton Raphson and Expectation maximization (EM) methods. Detection Theory - Hypothesis testing, Neyman-Pearson (N

title: Wireless Communications
description: Review of digital communication, optimal detection, overview of wireless communication generations and standards Channel modeling; Multipath propagation; pathloss and fading; types of fading; frequency and time selectivity Diversity techniques; spatial, time and frequency diversity; performance analysis of various diversity techniques MIMO communication systems; capacity analysis; MIMO receivers OFDM, massive MIMO, multiuser communication

title: Information Theory
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Foundations for Signal Processing and Communication
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Quantum Error-Correcting Codes
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Intro to Cognitive Science
description: Cognitive Science is a highly interdisciplinary field of study that seeks to understand how the mind works. In this course, we will discuss a diverse range of perspectives from philosophy, linguistics, psychology, neuroscience, and computer science, on how to unravel the mysteries of human cognition. Credits: 4 L-T-P: 3-1-0 (L = lecture hours, T = tutorial hours, P = practical hours) Prerequisite: None Textbook & Course Materials Recommended Texts & Other Readings: Lecture slides and supplementa

title: Introduction to Neural and Cognitive Modeling
description: Part I: Single-Neuron Level Models (1/3rd of the semester) Introduction to Neuroscience; Passive membrane models, Leaky Integrate and Fire (LIF) models, Hodgkin- Huxley model, Cable models of dendrites, Synapse model, Compartmental models. Part II: Network Level Models (1/3rd of the semester) Neural population codes; information representation; neural encoding and decoding; hierarchy and organization of sensory systems; Spiking Network models of sensory systems; Neuroplasticity and learning. Par

title: Behavioral Research & Experimental Design
description: 1. Introduction to Research Methods: Qualitative and Quantitative Approaches; Conducting Behavioural Research; Ethics in Research; Institute Review Board (IRB) Process 2. Starting on Research, Experimental Design: Hypothesis Testing, Type I and II errors, Hypothesis- based vs Exploratory Research, Operationalizing Research, Literature Review; Sampling, Types of variables and levels of Measurements, Designing an Experiment; Validity, Reliability and Cross- validation in Research 3. Types of Exper

title: Neuroinformatics
description: There are several methods we can use to record brain activity, including EEG, intracranial EEG, fMRI, MEG, etc. It takes a solid grasp of signal processing and other mathematical techniques to analyze these data. In this course, you will be introduced to these quantitative techniques for preprocessing neural data (removing noise, artifacts, etc.), processing neural data (using time-frequency decomposition and other methods), performing statistical analyses on these measures, and drawing conclusi

title: Introduction to Neuroeconomics
description: OBJECTIVE :Extend the understand of brain and behavior to economics and decision making. The course will be a seminar-style course covering the brain and behaviour pertaining to decision making as applied to economic theories. The course will begin with introduction to classical economics, the brain (brief intro to anatomy) structures attributed to learning, decision making, emotions, reward etc. The core part will be modern neuroeconomics and applications. By the end of the course the student w

title: Speech Analysis and Linguistics (SAL)
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Language Models and Agents
description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are transforming how tasks are automated and personalized across domains. As LLMs evolve into interactive agents, they are set to drive the next major breakthrough in AI. This course introduces the foundations of LLMs, key capabilities for task automation, and essential infrastructures for building LLM agents. We will explore applications such as generation, tool augmentation, web automation, healthcare, and scientific research, while also addressing current limitati

title: NLP for Healthcare
description: The course NLP for Healthcare aims to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of how Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques are applied to healthcare. The course will cover essential topics including medical text processing, clinical data analysis, patient-provider communication, and multimodal data integration. Students will learn to apply NLP methods to process clinical texts, electronic health records (EHRs), and patient notes, as well as build models for disease prediction,

title: Functional Analysis
description: UNIT-I Normed Space. Banach Space Finite Dimensional Normed Spaces and Subspaces Compactness and Finite Dimension (9 hours) UNIT-II Linear Operators Bounded and Continuous Linear Operators, Linear Functionals (7.5 hours) UNIT-III Linear Operators and Functionals on Finite Dimensional Spaces Normed Spaces of Operators. Dual Space (9 hours) Unit IV Inner Product Space. Hilbert Space, Properties of Inner Product Spaces Orthogonal Complements and Direct Sums, Orthonormal Sets and Sequences, Series R

title: Physics of Early Universe
description: Unit-1: Universe Observed: Expansion. Isotropy and homogeneity. Age. Cosmic microwave background Unit-2: Geometry and Dynamics: Universe in the eyes of Newton. Geometry. Mass and curvature. Freedman equations. Model universes: empty universe, matter or radiation dominated universe, multi- component universe Unit-3: Black-body radiation and the early history: Observation of CMB. Recombination and decoupling. Last scattering. Temperature fluctuations Unit-4: Very early history of the Universe: The

title: Introduction to Quantum Field Theory
description: Introduction: review of backgrounds, motivations for QFT Elements of Classical Field Theory: symmetries and No ether’s theorem Functional Formalism: path integral formalism, functional quantization, Feynman diagrams, quantization of scalar field, phi-4 theory S-matrix: scattering cross-section and decay rates, from Feynman diagrams to S-matrix Dirac Field: Dirac equation and its solutions, gamma matrices, quantization, Green's function Quantum Electrodynamics (QED): Feynman rules for QED, cross-

title: Dynamical processes in complex networks
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Topics in Nanosciences
description: Introduction to Nanoscience. (3L) Nanomaterials: Definitions and Scopes. Length Scales: Size Scales, Surface and Interface, Surface Energy, Coordination Numbers. Classification of nanomaterials: Clusters and Magic Numbers, Nanoparticles, and Colloids. Metal, Semiconductor, and Bio Nanomaterials. Properties of Nanomaterials. (9L) Electrons in Nanostructures. Discrete states vs. band structure: Effects of dimensionality and symmetry in nanostructures. Metal-to-nonmetal transition. Thermodynamics a

title: Finite Element Method
description: Module 1: Introduction to finite element method (FEM): Need for FEM, General finite element solution procedure, Boundary value problems: types of boundary conditions, Approximate solutionto boundary value problems: Method of weighted residuals, Module 2: General one-dimensional (1D) boundary value problem and its applications: Axial deformation of bars: Approximate solution using Rayleigh-Ritz method, Galerkin method, Axial Spring element. Applications: One-dimensional heat flow, Column buckling

title: Advanced Remote Sensing
description: Unit-1: Thermal Remote sensing: Introduction, Thermal IR Sensors- Characteristics of images; Physics of TIR: Radiation laws; Thermal Image Processing and Interpretation: Retrieval of Land Surface Emissivity, Land Surface Temperature and Applications. Unit -2. Hyperspectral Remote sensing: Introduction, Spectral Radiometry, Image acquisition and data characteristics, Information Extraction from Optical Image Data, Hyperspectral and Ultraspectral Information: Extraction Approaches, Applications. U

title: Spatial Informatics
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Cyber GIS: Geospatial Web and GeoBI
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Geospatial Technology for Disaster Risk Modelling
description: Unit-1: Basics of geospatial data analysis (3 hrs)  (a) Different types of geospatial data (Raster & Vector data format), (b) data acquisition and storage, (c) data preprocessing, (d) reanalysis (e) analysis ready and gridded climate data  Approach to retrieval of satellite data products and the concept of Algorithm Theoretical Basis Document (ATBD)  Basics of Hydrometeorological data, measurements, storage, retrieval, and mapping Unit-2: Flood risk and impact (12 hrs)  Urban, riverine and c

title: Readings from Hindi Literature
description: Graduate-level course in Product Design and Management.

title: Work, Entrepreneurship and Technology in Contemporary Societies.
description: Unit-1 Sociology of work:  Theories and basic concepts of work.  Historical discourses and practices around work.  Introduction to sectoral (agriculture, manufacturing, and service) understanding of work in contemporary markets. Unit-2 Diverse forms of work: Formal and informal work, and productive and reproductive work. Debt and bondage in labour markets; precarity and dignity within contemporary work arrangements. Role of social institutions (gender, caste, and technology) in shaping work a

title: Rethinking Corruption in India
description: This course offers a critical and historically grounded inquiry into the concept of corruption in India. Rather than treating corruption as simply a moral lapse or a problem of weak institutions, the course explores how corruption is shaped by deeper processes: economic exchange, caste hierarchies, political ideologies, colonial legacies, and the logic of state formation. Students will interrogate the idea of corruption as a moving and contested category — one that has served to legitimise some

title: Advanced Computer Architecture
description: Covers advanced topics in computer architecture including memory hierarchy, pipelining, instruction-level parallelism, cache design, multicore processors, and hardware-software co-design for high-performance computing.

title: Deep Learning: Theory and Practices
description: Provides a rigorous treatment of deep learning covering feedforward networks, CNNs, RNNs, optimization, regularisation, and modern architectures with emphasis on both theory and practical implementation.

title: Fairness, Privacy and Ethics in AI
description: Examines ethical dimensions of AI systems including algorithmic fairness, data privacy, bias mitigation, transparency, accountability, and regulatory frameworks for responsible AI deployment.

title: Introduction to Remote Sensing
description: Introduces principles of remote sensing including electromagnetic radiation, sensor types, satellite data processing, GIS integration, and applications in environmental and disaster management.

title: Real-Time Embedded Systems
description: Studies real-time systems theory and RTOS design including task modelling, scheduling algorithms (RM, EDF), multiprocessor scheduling, shared resource management, and implementation on RTOS platforms.

title: Software Quality Engineering
description: Covers systematic approaches to software quality including testing methodologies, static analysis, code reviews, continuous integration, formal verification, defect measurement, and process quality standards.

title: Data Structures & Algorithms for Problem Solving
description: A foundational PG course covering fundamental and advanced data structures—linked lists, trees, AVL trees, heaps, hash tables, tries—and core algorithms including recursion, searching, sorting, dynamic programming, and graph traversal, with emphasis on implementation and problem-solving.

title: Software Systems Development
description: A hands-on PG foundation course covering Linux and shell scripting, web programming (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Python), database programming, networking and security concepts, and basics of SDLC—designed to give MTech students working knowledge of tools to build software systems.
